event_text: Llywelyn ap Seisyll becomes a leading ruler in Wales.
Gruffydd ap Llywelyn becomes ruler of Gwynedd and later unites much of Wales.
Gruffydd ap Llywelyn is killed after conflict with Harold Godwinson.
Norman pressure begins to reshape the Welsh Marches after the conquest of England.
Welsh revolts challenge Norman power across parts of Wales.
Welsh forces defeat Norman forces at Crug Mawr.
Rhys ap Gruffydd holds a celebrated cultural gathering at Cardigan, often linked to the eisteddfod tradition.
Llywelyn the Great defeats his uncle Dafydd and becomes dominant in Gwynedd.
The Council of Aberdyfi recognises Llywelyn the Great’s leadership among Welsh rulers.
The Treaty of Worcester confirms succession arrangements for Dafydd ap Llywelyn.
Llywelyn the Great dies and is succeeded by Dafydd ap Llywelyn.
Llywelyn ap Gruffudd is recognised by Welsh nobles as Prince of Wales.
The Treaty of Montgomery recognises Llywelyn ap Gruffudd as Prince of Wales.
Llywelyn ap Gruffudd is killed, ending the last major native principality of Wales.
Dafydd ap Gruffydd is executed after the final defeat of native Welsh rule.
The Statute of Rhuddlan establishes English administration in conquered Wales.
Edward of Caernarfon is invested as Prince of Wales.
The Black Death reaches Wales and causes major population loss.
Owain Glyndŵr is proclaimed Prince of Wales, beginning a major Welsh revolt against English rule.
Owain Glyndŵr holds a parliament at Machynlleth.
Owain Glyndŵr disappears from the historical record.
Henry Tudor is born at Pembroke Castle.
Henry Tudor lands in Wales before defeating Richard III at Bosworth.
The first Laws in Wales Act integrates Wales into the English legal system.
The second Laws in Wales Act completes the legal union process.
William Salesbury’s Welsh translation of the New Testament is published.
William Morgan’s Welsh Bible is published, helping preserve the Welsh language.
Royalist resistance in Wales is defeated during the Second English Civil War.
Nonconformist religious life grows after the Act of Toleration.
The Welsh Methodist revival begins to gather pace under leaders such as Howell Harris.
The Industrial Revolution begins transforming south Wales through iron, coal and ports.
The Cyfarthfa Ironworks opens near Merthyr Tydfil.
The last invasion of Britain ends near Fishguard.
Richard Trevithick’s steam locomotive runs at Penydarren.
The Merthyr Rising begins amid industrial hardship.
The Welsh Calvinistic Methodists establish a separate denomination.
The Merthyr Rising becomes a major working-class protest in industrial Wales.
The first major Rebecca Riot at Efailwen begins a period of protest against tollgates and rural hardship.
The Reports of the Commissioners of Inquiry into Education in Wales spark major debate.
The first Welsh-language newspaper Baner Cymru grows in influence during the nineteenth century.
Welsh settlers establish a colony in Patagonia.
Aberystwyth University opens as the first university college in Wales.
The Welsh Sunday Closing Act restricts Sunday alcohol sales in Wales.
County councils are established in Wales under local government reforms.
The University of Wales is founded.
Cardiff gains city status.
The National Library of Wales is founded at Aberystwyth.
Prince Edward is invested as Prince of Wales at Caernarfon Castle.
Welsh communities are deeply affected by the First World War.
David Lloyd George becomes the first Welsh-born prime minister of the United Kingdom.
Plaid Cymru is founded.
The Penyberth protest opposes the bombing school on the Llŷn Peninsula.
Welsh industry, ports and communities contribute to the Second World War.
Cardiff is officially recognised as the capital of Wales.
The Tryweryn valley is selected for flooding to create a reservoir for Liverpool.
The Welsh Office is established.
Gwynfor Evans wins the Carmarthen by-election for Plaid Cymru.
The Welsh Language Act gives limited legal recognition to Welsh.
Prince Charles is invested as Prince of Wales at Caernarfon Castle.
A referendum rejects Welsh devolution.
S4C, the Welsh-language television channel, begins broadcasting.
The Welsh Language Act strengthens the public status of Welsh.
A referendum narrowly supports Welsh devolution.
The National Assembly for Wales opens.
The Government of Wales Act increases devolved powers.
Wales votes for greater law-making powers for the Assembly.
The National Assembly is renamed Senedd Cymru, the Welsh Parliament.
